title: भगवद्गीता अध्याय ६ - ध्यानयोग
devanagariText:
॥ अथ षष्ठोऽध्यायः - ध्यानयोगः ॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः।
स सन्न्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः॥१॥

यं सन्न्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव।
न ह्यसन्न्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन॥२॥

आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते।
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते॥३॥

यदा हि नेन्द्रियार्थेषु न कर्मस्वनुषज्जते।
सर्वसङ्कल्पसन्न्यासी योगारूढस्तदोच्यते॥४॥

उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत्।
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः॥५॥

बन्धुरात्मात्मनस्तस्य येनात्मैवात्मना जितः।
अनात्मनस्तु शत्रुत्वे वर्तेतात्मैव शत्रुवत्॥६॥

जितात्मनः प्रशान्तस्य परमात्मा समाहितः।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु तथा मानापमानयोः॥७॥

ज्ञानविज्ञानतृप्तात्मा कूटस्थो विजितेन्द्रियः।
युक्त इत्युच्यते योगी समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः॥८॥

सुहृन्मित्रार्युदासीनमध्यस्थद्वेष्यबन्धुषु।
साधुष्वपि च पापेषु समबुद्धिर्विशिष्यते॥९॥

योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः।
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः॥१०॥

शुचौ देशे प्रतिष्ठाप्य स्थिरमासनमात्मनः।
नात्युच्छ्रितं नातिनीचं चैलाजिनकुशोत्तरम्॥११॥

तत्रैकाग्रं मनः कृत्वा यतचित्तेन्द्रियक्रियः।
उपविश्यासने युञ्ज्याद्योगमात्मविशुद्धये॥१२॥

समं कायशिरोग्रीवं धारयन्नचलं स्थिरः।
सम्प्रेक्ष्य नासिकाग्रं स्वं दिशश्चानवलोकयन्॥१३॥

प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः।
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तो युक्त आसीत मत्परः॥१४॥

युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी नियतमानसः।
शान्तिं निर्वाणपरमां मत्संस्थामधिगच्छति॥१५॥

नात्यश्नतस्तु योगोऽस्ति न चैकान्तमनश्नतः।
न चातिस्वप्नशीलस्य जाग्रतो नैव चार्जुन॥१६॥

युक्ताहारविहारस्य युक्तचेष्टस्य कर्मसु।
युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा॥१७॥

यदा विनियतं चित्तमात्मन्येवावतिष्ठते।
निःस्पृहः सर्वकामेभ्यो युक्त इत्युच्यते तदा॥१८॥

यथा दीपो निवातस्थो नेङ्गते सोपमा स्मृता।
योगिनो यतचित्तस्य युञ्जतो योगमात्मनः॥१९॥

यत्रोपरमते चित्तं निरुद्धं योगसेवया।
यत्र चैवात्मनात्मानं पश्यन्नात्मनि तुष्यति॥२०॥

सुखमात्यन्तिकं यत्तद्बुद्धिग्राह्यमतीन्द्रियम्।
वेत्ति यत्र न चैवायं स्थितश्चलति तत्त्वतः॥२१॥

यं लब्ध्वा चापरं लाभं मन्यते नाधिकं ततः।
यस्मिन्स्थितो न दुःखेन गुरुणापि विचाल्यते॥२२॥

तं विद्याद्दुःखसंयोगवियोगं योगसंज्ञितम्।
स निश्चयेन योक्तव्यो योगोऽनिर्विण्णचेतसा॥२३॥

अर्जुन उवाच —
योऽयं योगस्त्वया प्रोक्तः साम्येन मधुसूदन।
एतस्याहं न पश्यामि चञ्चलत्वात्स्थितिं स्थिराम्॥३३॥

चञ्चलं हि मनः कृष्ण प्रमाथि बलवद्दृढम्।
तस्याहं निग्रहं मन्ये वायोरिव सुदुष्करम्॥३४॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम्।
अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते॥३५॥

असंयतात्मना योगो दुष्प्राप इति मे मतिः।
वश्यात्मना तु यतता शक्योऽवाप्तुमुपायतः॥३६॥

अर्जुन उवाच —
अयतिः श्रद्धयोपेतो योगाच्चलितमानसः।
अप्राप्य योगसंसिद्धिं कां गतिं कृष्ण गच्छति॥३७॥

श्रीभगवानुवाच —
पार्थ नैवेह नामुत्र विनाशस्तस्य विद्यते।
न हि कल्याणकृत्कश्चिद्दुर्गतिं तात गच्छति॥४०॥

योगिनामपि सर्वेषां मद्गतेनान्तरात्मना।
श्रद्धावान्भजते यो मां स मे युक्ततमो मतः॥४७॥

॥ इति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे
श्रीकृष्णार्जुनसंवादे ध्यानयोगो नाम षष्ठोऽध्यायः ॥
transliteration:
|| Atha Shashtho'dhyayah - Dhyana Yogah ||

Shri Bhagavan Uvacha —
Anashritah Karma-Phalam Karyam Karma Karoti Yah.
Sa Sannyasi Cha Yogi Cha Na Nir-Agnir-Na Cha-Akriyah.||1||

Yam Sannyasam-Iti Prahur-Yogam Tam Viddhi Pandava.
Na Hy-Asannyasta-Sankalpo Yogi Bhavati Kashchana.||2||

Arurukshor-Muner-Yogam Karma Karanam-Uchyate.
Yoga-Arudhasya Tasya-Iva Shamah Karanam-Uchyate.||3||

Yada Hi Na-Indriya-Artheshu Na Karmasv-Anushajjate.
Sarva-Sankalpa-Sannyasi Yoga-Arudhas-Tado-Uchyate.||4||

Uddhared-Atmana-Atmanam Na-Atmanam-Avasadayet.
Atma-Iva Hy-Atmano Bandhur-Atma-Iva Ripur-Atmanah.||5||

Bandhur-Atma-Atmanas-Tasya Yena-Atma-Iva-Atmana Jitah.
Anatmanas-Tu Shatrutve Varteta-Atma-Iva Shatruvat.||6||

Jita-Atmanah Prashantasya Parama-Atma Samahitah.
Shito-Ushna-Sukha-Duhkheshu Tatha Mana-Apamanayoh.||7||

Jnana-Vijnana-Tripta-Atma Kutastho Vijite-Indriyah.
Yukta Ity-Uchyate Yogi Sama-Loshta-Ashma-Kanchanah.||8||

Yogi Yunjita Satatam-Atmanam Rahasi Sthitah.
Ekaki Yata-Chitta-Atma Nirashir-Aparigrahah.||10||

Shuchau Deshe Pratishthapya Sthiram-Asanam-Atmanah.
Na-Aty-Uchchhritam Na-Ati-Nicham Chaila-Ajina-Kusho-Uttaram.||11||

Tatra-Ikagram Manah Kritva Yata-Chitta-Indriya-Kriyah.
Upavishya-Asane Yunjyad-Yogam-Atma-Vishuddhaye.||12||

Samam Kaya-Shiro-Grivam Dharayann-Achalam Sthirah.
Samprekshya Nasika-Agram Svam Dishah-Cha-An-Avalokayan.||13||

Na-Aty-Ashnatas-Tu Yogo'sti Na Chaika-Antam-Anashnatah.
Na Cha-Ati-Svapna-Shilasya Jagrato Naiva Cha-Arjuna.||16||

Yukta-Ahara-Viharasya Yukta-Cheshtasya Karmasu.
Yukta-Svapna-Avabodhasya Yogo Bhavati Duhkha-Ha.||17||

Yatha Dipo Nivata-Stho Ne-Ingate So-Upama Smrita.
Yogino Yata-Chittasya Yunjato Yogam-Atmanah.||19||

Chanchalam Hi Manah Krishna Pramathi Balavad Dridham.
Tasya-Aham Nigraham Manye Vayor-Iva Su-Dushkaram.||34||

Shri Bhagavan Uvacha —
Asamshayam Maha-Baho Mano Dur-Nigraham Chalam.
Abhyasena Tu Kaunteya Vairagyena Cha Grihyate.||35||

Partha Naiveha Na-Amutra Vinashas-Tasya Vidyate.
Na Hi Kalyana-Krit-Kashchid-Durgatim Tata Gachchhati.||40||

Yoginam-Api Sarvesham Mad-Gatena-Antar-Atmana.
Shraddha-Van Bhajate Yo Mam Sa Me Yuktatamo Matah.||47||

|| Iti Shrimad-Bhagavad-Gitasu-Upanishatsu Brahma-Vidyayam Yoga-Shastre
Shri-Krishna-Arjuna-Samvade Dhyana-Yogo Nama Shashtho'dhyayah ||
hindiMeaning: षष्ठ अध्याय 'ध्यानयोग' में श्रीकृष्ण ध्यान और आत्म-संयम की विधि बताते हैं। सबसे पहले कहते हैं कि जो कर्मफल की आशा न रखकर कर्तव्य कर्म करता है, वही सच्चा सन्न्यासी और योगी है। 'उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं' — मनुष्य को अपने आप को स्वयं ऊपर उठाना चाहिए, आत्मा ही अपना बन्धु है और आत्मा ही अपना शत्रु। ध्यान की विधि विस्तार से बताई — शुद्ध स्थान पर आसन बिछाकर, शरीर, सिर और गर्दन को सीधा रखकर, नासिका के अग्रभाग पर दृष्टि रखकर ध्यान करें। संतुलित आहार-विहार वाले का योग दुःखनाशक होता है। अर्जुन कहते हैं — मन चंचल है, वायु को रोकने जैसा कठिन है। कृष्ण कहते हैं — अभ्यास और वैराग्य से मन को वश में किया जा सकता है। जो भक्तिपूर्वक मुझे भजता है, वह सबसे श्रेष्ठ योगी है।
benefits: Provides practical guidance on meditation techniques
Teaches the art of self-mastery and mind control
Contains the powerful teaching: Atmaiva Hyatmano Bandhuh
Explains balanced living (Yukta Ahara Vihara) for spiritual success
Assures that no spiritual effort ever goes to waste
Declares the devotee as the highest yogi
Traditionally associated with deep inner peace and mental stability
description: Dedicated to Lord Krishna, the Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6 - Dhyana Yoga (भगवद्गीता अध्याय ६ - ध्यानयोग) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. Rooted in Bhagavad Gita - Mahabharata, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to assure that no spiritual effort ever goes to waste as well as teach the art of self-mastery and mind control. Additionally, it is known to declare the devotee as the highest yogi. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Janmashtami. At 47 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 22 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6 - Dhyana Yoga in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6 Dhyana Yoga in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. 47 verses on meditation, self-mastery, and balanced living.

seoFaqs.question: What is Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6 — Dhyana Yoga — about?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6 (ध्यानयोग — Dhyana Yoga, also called आत्मसंयमयोग — Atma Samyama Yoga, the Yoga of Self-Restraint) is a 47-verse teaching by Lord Krishna to Arjuna on the formal practice of meditation. The chapter opens with the definition of the true sannyasi as one who performs duty without depending on its fruits (6.1), establishes that the mind alone is the friend or enemy of the Self (atmaiva hyatmano bandhuratmaiva ripuratmanah — 6.5-6.6), prescribes detailed instructions for the meditative posture, place, diet and discipline (6.10-6.17), describes the steady mind as a lamp that does not flicker in a windless place (yatha dipo nivata-stho — 6.19), addresses Arjuna's despair that the restless mind is harder to control than the wind (chanchalam hi manah krishna — 6.34), and concludes with Krishna's supreme declaration that of all yogis, the bhakta who worships Him with faith is the highest. (Source: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6; classical commentaries.)

seoFaqs.question: What posture and discipline does Krishna prescribe for meditation in Chapter 6?
seoFaqs.answer: In verses 6.10-6.17 Krishna gives the most systematic meditation instructions in the Gita: (1) shuchau deshe — choose a clean and secluded place; (2) sthiram asanam atmanah — establish a firm seat, neither too high nor too low, covered with kusha grass, deer skin and cloth (6.11); (3) sit with body, neck and head erect and motionless, gazing at the tip of the nose, not looking around (6.13); (4) practice with a controlled mind, free from fear, established in brahmacharya, devoted to Me (6.14); (5) maintain moderation — yuktahara-viharasya — moderate eating, sleeping, recreation and work (6.16-6.17). The yogi who practices in this disciplined way attains the supreme peace that culminates in Me (6.15). These verses are the foundation of all subsequent Hindu meditation manuals and parallel Patanjali's Yoga Sutras 2.46-49 on asana and pranayama. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 6.10-6.17.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous flame-in-windless-place verse in Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6?
seoFaqs.answer: The flame-in-windless-place verse — Bhagavad Gita 6.19 — is one of the most beautiful similes for meditative samadhi: yatha dipo nivata-stho nengate sopama smrita / yogino yata-chittasya yunjato yogam atmanah — "Just as a lamp placed in a windless place does not flicker, such is the simile remembered for the yogi of controlled mind, practising the yoga of the Self." In ordinary life the mind flickers like a flame in the wind of desires, fears and impressions. Through meditative practice the wind of mental modifications (vrittis) is stilled, and the inner light of consciousness shines steady, unmoving and bright. This verse is invoked across all Hindu meditation traditions — Advaita, Yoga, Bhakti, Tantra — as the iconic image of citta-vritti-nirodha (cessation of mental modifications). (Source: Bhagavad Gita 6.19; Patanjali Yoga Sutras 1.2.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Krishna address Arjuna's complaint that the mind is uncontrollable?
seoFaqs.answer: In verse 6.34 Arjuna voices the universal complaint of every meditator: chanchalam hi manah krishna pramathi balavad dridham / tasyaham nigraham manye vayoriva sudushkaram — "O Krishna, the mind is restless, turbulent, powerful and obstinate; to control it I think is as difficult as controlling the wind." Krishna agrees in 6.35 — asamshayam mahabaho mano durnigraham chalam — "Without doubt, O mighty-armed, the mind is restless and hard to control." But, He continues, abhyasena tu kaunteya vairagyena cha grihyate — "yet by repeated practice (abhyasa) and dispassion (vairagya), it can be restrained." This verse 6.35 is the most-quoted meditation pep-talk in Hindu literature and is the direct source of Patanjali Yoga Sutra 1.12 (abhyasa-vairagyabhyam tan-nirodhah). The remedy is steady, patient practice combined with cultivated detachment. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 6.34-6.36.)

seoFaqs.question: What happens to a yogi who falls from the path — the yoga-bhrashta of Chapter 6?
seoFaqs.answer: Arjuna asks (6.37-6.39) the poignant question: what happens to one who has faith but cannot perfect yoga in this life — does such a yoga-bhrashta (one fallen from yoga) perish like a torn cloud? Krishna's reply (6.40-6.45) is one of the most reassuring passages in the Gita: na hi kalyana-krit kashchid durgatim tata gachchati — "never does one who does good come to a bad end." The yoga-bhrashta is reborn in the homes of the pure and prosperous (shuchinam shrimatam gehe), or in a family of wise yogis (yoginam eva kule) — a birth difficult to obtain in this world. There he regains the spiritual intelligence cultivated in his former body and strives further with greater effort, and after many lives of practice, attains the supreme goal. No spiritual effort is ever wasted (6.40). This is the foundational Gita doctrine of cumulative spiritual progress across lifetimes. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 6.37-6.45.)

seoFaqs.question: Who does Krishna call the highest yogi at the end of Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6?
seoFaqs.answer: The chapter closes (6.46-6.47) with Krishna's hierarchical ranking of yogis: tapasvibhyo'dhiko yogi jnanibhyo'pi mato'dhikah / karmibhyash chadhiko yogi tasmad yogi bhavarjuna — "The yogi is greater than the ascetic (tapasvi), greater even than the man of knowledge (jnani), and greater than the ritualist (karmi); therefore, O Arjuna, become a yogi." Then comes the supreme verse 6.47: yoginam api sarvesham mad-gatena antaratmana / shraddhavan bhajate yo mam sa me yuktatamo matah — "Of all yogis, the one who worships Me with absorbed inner soul and faith, that one I consider the most united (yuktatama)." This verse establishes that bhakti yoga is the crowning expression of all yoga — and serves as the bridge into the bhakti-centred Chapters 7-12 of the Gita. (Source: Bhagavad Gita 6.46-6.47.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6 relate to Patanjali's Yoga Sutras?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6 and Patanjali's Yoga Sutras share the same vocabulary and many parallel teachings, but represent distinct yoga traditions converging on common practical wisdom. Both texts use yoga, dhyana, samadhi, abhyasa, vairagya, citta, vritti and chanchala-manas in nearly identical senses. Gita 6.35 (abhyasena tu kaunteya vairagyena cha grihyate) directly anticipates Yoga Sutra 1.12 (abhyasa-vairagyabhyam tan-nirodhah). Gita's posture instructions (6.11-6.13) parallel Yoga Sutra 2.46-49 on asana and pranayama. However, the Gita differs in three important ways: (1) it is theistic — culminating in devotion to Krishna (6.47), whereas Patanjali's Ishvara is a special Purusha; (2) it integrates karma yoga and jnana yoga as inseparable from dhyana yoga; (3) it is contextualised within dharma and svadharma (one's own duty in life). Both texts complement each other and are studied together in classical Yoga sadhana. (Source: Bhagavad Gita Chapter 6; Patanjali Yoga Sutras Sadhana Pada.)
